title: resolveDecoderProgressiveDetail disables progressive for single-pass sessions that have an explicit progressiveDetail set
description: The condition on line 434 reads `!(options.progressionTarget !== 'final' || options.emitEveryPass)`, which is De Morgan equivalent to `options.progressionTarget === 'final' && !options.emitEveryPass`. However, this check fires even when `options.progressiveDetail` is defined (the surrounding `if` only checks `progressiveDetail === undefined`). An explicit `progressiveDetail` such as `lastPasses` must subscribe to libjxl progressive events even when emitEveryPass=false — the comment on line 431 actually says this — but the condition can also short-circuit to 0 for progressionTarget='pass' with emitEveryPass=false when progressiveDetail is absent, which silently requests a non-progressive decode while callers expect at least one pass event.
code_excerpt: if (options.progressiveDetail === undefined && !(options.progressionTarget !== "final" || options.emitEveryPass)) return 0;
evidence: The outer guard `options.progressiveDetail === undefined` means the short-circuit only fires when progressiveDetail is NOT defined, exactly matching the comment. When progressiveDetail is defined the entire `if` is skipped and the function proceeds to map it. The logic is correct as written.

title: Progressive decoder pushes batchBytes from before-drain queue but drains chunks inside the batch loop, causing woff to drift from actual bytes written
description: At line 1515 `batchBytes` is captured as `this.queuedBytes` before the copy loop. Inside the loop, `this.queuedBytes` is decremented per chunk (line 1533). If the chunk sizes sum to less than `batchBytes` (because another consumer or re-entrant call modified `queuedBytes` — theoretically impossible in single-thread JS, but practically because the final `null` sentinel may not be present), the WASM push call at line 1539 passes `batchBytes` (original total) rather than the actual `woff` bytes written into the heap buffer. This means extra uninitialized bytes are fed to the decoder. In normal operation this is consistent; it becomes wrong if `woff !== batchBytes` which cannot happen in the current single-threaded path, but any future change to the queue draining could silently corrupt decode. The safe fix is `decPush(dec, chunkBufPtr, woff)` instead of `decPush(dec, chunkBufPtr, batchBytes)`.
code_excerpt:
result = decPush(dec, chunkBufPtr, batchBytes);
          if (result < 0) throw new Error(`JXL decode error: ${decError(dec)}`);
evidence: Code confirmed at lines 1515 and 1539: `batchBytes` is captured before the drain loop and passed to `decPush`, while `woff` tracks actual bytes written. In the single-threaded JS model these always match today, but passing `woff` is strictly safer and self-documenting. Latent correctness risk on any future queue-draining refactor.

title: Warm-up in benchmarkModule fires only once across all files in all scenarios, not once per file
description: The `warmed` flag is set the first time ANY file in any scenario is processed, meaning only the very first file of the first scenario gets a warm-up run.
evidence: The `warmed` flag is declared outside the per-scenario loop, so only the very first file warms up regardless of scenario count. The intent (JIT warm-up once per module load) is achieved, but if per-scenario warm-up was intended the flag placement is wrong. The secondary concern — `warmed` never resets across multiple `benchmarkModule` calls — is also real since it lives in the closure.

title: JXTC tile-seam blend formula is asymmetric: left tile heavily weighted (179/256) and right tile lightly weighted (77/256), then both pixels set to the same blended value
description: The blend formula `(l*179 + r*77 + 128) >> 8` assigns weight 179/256 ≈ 0.70 to the left pixel and 77/256 ≈ 0.30 to the right pixel. Both the left and right pixel are then written back to the same blended value.
evidence: Code confirmed at lines 1786-1788. Both pL and pR are set to the same asymmetrically-weighted blend. A correct seam blend would assign `pL = lerp(l,r,0.25)` and `pR = lerp(l,r,0.75)`, giving each side a different weighted value. The current code writes the same value to both sides, which merely shifts the seam rather than smoothing it.

title: Horizontal seam blend in JXTC uses same asymmetric 179/77 weighting, both rows written to identical value
description: The horizontal seam loop uses `t` and `b` for top/bottom pixel values with the same asymmetric 179/77 weighting. Both pT and pB are set to the same blended value.
evidence: Code confirmed at lines 1803-1808. Same structural problem as logic-4: both pT and pB receive the same asymmetric blend value instead of separate lerp values biased toward their respective sides. The variable `b` shadows the outer blend name but this is just a naming confusion, not a separate bug.

title: encodeRgba8 passes the same metaPtr three times for all three metadata slots (ICC, EXIF, XMP)
description: When metadata bytes are present, the function allocates a single `metaPtr` and passes this same pointer and size for all three metadata fields: icc_ptr, exif_ptr, and xmp_ptr.
evidence: Code confirmed at lines 143-148: single `metaPtr` with identical ptr+size passed for all three metadata slots. This is a copy-paste error — the bridge expects separate ICC/EXIF/XMP buffers. The PGO training corpus will encode incorrect metadata in the `encode-container` scenario, producing a suboptimal PGO profile for metadata-aware encode paths.

title: pgo-train.mjs calls pathToFileUrl() before it is defined — function declaration hoisting does not apply to functions defined later in the module
description: Line 14 calls `pathToFileUrl(moduleJs)` at the module's top-level execution scope before the function definition at line 245.
evidence: `pathToFileUrl` is a `function` declaration (not an arrow/const), so hoisting applies in JS — it is accessible at line 14 regardless of textual position. The finder self-corrected within the description but still flagged it. The Windows path encoding `file:///C:/foo` analysis in the description is correct — no bug exists.

title: passOrdinal emitted on progress events is flushCount-1 (zero-based), but on final it is flushCount (also zero-based counting the final as a pass), creating an inconsistent ordinal across event types
description: Progress events use `passOrdinal: flushCount - 1` while final events use `passOrdinal: flushCount`.
code_excerpt:
passOrdinal: flushCount - 1,
...
            passOrdinal: flushCount,
evidence: Code confirmed: flushCount is incremented to 1 before the progress event emit, so the DC pass gets passOrdinal=0 (correct). The final event gets passOrdinal=flushCount which equals total flush count, not last-pass index. For a 2-pass file: progress events have ordinals 0,1 and final has ordinal 2 — the final's ordinal is one past the last progress ordinal, which is semantically inconsistent for consumers using ordinal to identify the highest-quality pass.

title: jxtcNonTileRanges reads tilesX at offset 20 and tilesY at offset 24, informational tileCount overflow check
description: The JXTC header reads are correct per the C++ layout. The `Number.isSafeInteger` guard is present. This is informational.
code_excerpt:
const tilesX = dv.getUint32(20, true);
  const tilesY = dv.getUint32(24, true);
evidence: Code at lines 2694-2699 confirmed correct: h32[5]=tilesX at offset 20, h32[6]=tilesY at offset 24 match the C++ layout. `Number.isSafeInteger(tileCount)` guard is already present at line 2699. The minimum-size check at line 2692 correctly allows 1-tile containers. No logic error.

title: LibjxlEncoder.chunks() byte total check will throw on zero-pixel edge case
description: Informational — the constructor already rejects zero dimensions via expectedPixelBytes.
code_excerpt: if (this.queuedPixelBytes !== this.pixelByteTotal) {
evidence: The finder self-assessed as informational in the description. `expectedPixelBytes` throws for width/height <=0 in the constructor, so the queuedPixelBytes===0 path is only reachable if zero pixels were pushed for a valid-dimension image, which is correct error behavior. No bug.

title: distanceFromQuality returns distance=1 for quality=null instead of the JXL lossless distance (0.0) or documented default
description: When `quality` is null, `distanceFromQuality` returns 1 (approximately quality 95), which may surprise callers expecting lossless when both distance and quality are null.
code_excerpt:
function distanceFromQuality(quality: number | null): number {
  if (quality === null) return 1;
evidence: Code confirmed at line 2647. When both `options.distance` and `options.quality` are null, the encoder silently uses distance=1 (lossy ~95 quality). The EncoderOptions interface allows both to be null but there is no JSDoc warning about this lossy default. A caller who intends lossless by passing no quality/distance will get lossy output.

title: The else-if branch 'inputClosed && result === 0' after result===2 done check — finder concluded not dead
description: After the main decode loop body, if result===0 and inputClosed is true, line 1635 throws an error. Finder self-corrected: this is a valid safety-net, not dead code.
code_excerpt:
} else if (inputClosed) {
          throw new Error(`JXL decode error: ${decError(dec)}`);
evidence: The finder explicitly concluded in the description that this is not a dead branch but a valid error-safety path. The branch correctly handles the case where the decoder keeps returning NEED_MORE after close() was called, indicating a corrupt stream.

title: No test for the 'rgb8' format path in expectedPixelBytes
description: The `expectedPixelBytes` function uses `channels = format === 'rgb8' ? 3 : 4` but no tests push exactly `w*h*3` bytes for rgb8 to verify end-to-end correctness.
code_excerpt: const channels = format === "rgb8" ? 3 : 4;
evidence: The rgb8 channel count branch is real. A missing test for the rgb8 encode path means a regression (e.g. accidentally using 4 channels) would not be caught. This is a genuine test-coverage gap, not a false positive.

title: JXTC tile container index cursor uses uint32_t which can overflow for containers >4 GB total
description: The `cursor` variable is uint32_t; on WASM32 the heap is bounded by 32-bit address space so >4 GB is impossible in practice.
code_excerpt: uint32_t cursor = static_cast<uint32_t>(header_bytes + index_bytes);
evidence: The finder self-assessed as low confidence and noted the WASM32 static_assert makes >4 GB impossible. `total_size` is size_t (32-bit on WASM32) and the allocator would fail before cursor could overflow. No practical risk.

title: Progressive decoder batch write uses pre-drain queuedBytes as size argument — informational, finder concluded safe
description: batchBytes vs woff mismatch — finder concluded in the description that in the single-threaded JS model woff always equals batchBytes for actual data chunks.
code_excerpt:
const batchBytes = this.queuedBytes;
          result = decPush(dec, chunkBufPtr, batchBytes);
evidence: This is the same underlying issue as logic-2 but the finder concluded it is safe in the current model and marked it informational. Verdict follows logic-2 (confirmed) for the latent risk but as a duplicate finding this should be merged with logic-2.

title: pickDownsample returns the first factor where downsampled image >= target — finder concluded logic is sound
description: pickDownsample iterates [8,4,2] and returns first factor where downsampled size >= target. Finder concluded the logic is correct for picking the coarsest downsample that still meets minimum resolution.
evidence: The finder explicitly concluded the logic is sound in the description. Iterating from highest factor to lowest and returning first match that is >= target correctly selects the coarsest adequate downsample. No bug.

title: JXL_PGO_MODULE_JS environment variable is imported directly as a dynamic module without any validation
description: The script reads the file path from JXL_PGO_MODULE_JS and calls `import(pathToFileUrl(moduleJs))` with no sanitization.
evidence: Code confirmed at lines 6 and 14. This is a build-time developer script, not user-facing code, so the threat model is CI misconfiguration or supply-chain attack rather than end-user exploitation. However, the finding is real: an attacker who controls env vars in CI could execute arbitrary JS in the build process.

title: benchmarkModule imports an arbitrary JS file path supplied by the caller with no validation
description: The exported `benchmarkModule` function calls `import(pathToFileURL(moduleJs).href)` where `moduleJs` comes from CLI arguments without sanitization.
code_excerpt:
export async function benchmarkModule({ moduleJs, moduleWasm, scenarios, reps = 3 }) {
  const imported = await import(pathToFileURL(moduleJs).href);
evidence: Code confirmed at line 57. Same class as security-1 — build-time developer script. CLI args are not user input in production, but this pattern is unsafe in shared CI environments. The finding is valid for its threat model.

title: LIBJXL_REPO environment variable is passed unsanitized to git clone as the remote URL
description: config.libjxlRepo comes directly from process.env.LIBJXL_REPO and is used as the remote URL argument to git clone.
code_excerpt: libjxlRepo: process.env.LIBJXL_REPO ?? "https://github.com/libjxl/libjxl.git",
evidence: The env-var default fallback to the real GitHub URL is present, but an override (valid in CI for mirror repos) could be set to a malicious URL. Build scripts that clone external repos are a classic supply-chain vector. The LIBJXL_COMMIT pin mitigates only after checkout.

title: LIBJXL_REPO environment variable is passed unsanitized to git clone in build.mjs
description: Same pattern as security-3 in the main build script.
code_excerpt: libjxlRepo: process.env.LIBJXL_REPO ?? "https://github.com/libjxl/libjxl.git",
evidence: Duplicate of security-3 in build.mjs. Additionally DOCKER_BIN and EMSDK_IMAGE env vars are passed unsanitized to docker command arguments per the description, widening the injection surface beyond just git clone.

title: expandGlob accepts glob patterns from the manifest JSON without preventing directory traversal above the manifest root
description: The `expandGlob(root, pattern)` function does not verify that resolved paths stay within `root`.
evidence: Code confirmed: `resolve(root, normalized)` with a pattern like `../../secret` produces a path outside root, and there is no subsequent check that the resolved path starts with `root`. A malicious or compromised `pgo-manifest.json` in the corpus could read arbitrary files.

title: PGO lock file is read and parsed without integrity verification
description: The existing lock file's `scenarios` array is used directly without cryptographic verification.
evidence: The lock file is an artifact on disk with no HMAC or signature. An attacker who can write to the dist/ directory (compromised CI artifact step) can inject arbitrary scenarios. The risk is real in shared CI environments.

title: BoxDownscaleRgba8 does not validate that sw, sh are non-zero before ceiling division path
description: No check that sw>0 and sh>0 before the general-case ceiling division path; extremely large sw/sh values could overflow uint32_t in `(dy*sh+dh-1u)/dh`.
evidence: Code confirmed at line 1095: only dw==0 and dh==0 are guarded. If sw==0 the fast-path modulo `sw % dw` would give 0/dw=0 (safe), but the general path `(dy*sh)/dh` with sh==0 gives 0 for all y0/y1, so inner loops silently do nothing. The overflow concern at `(dy+1u)*sh + dh-1u` is real if both dy and sh are near UINT32_MAX — callers pass these from TS integer fields where validation is partial.

title: runEmscripten uses shell:true on Windows for .bat files, enabling shell meta-character injection from attacker-controlled paths
description: The `runEmscripten` function passes `shell: true` for .bat commands on Win32, and the command comes from the EMSDK env var.
evidence: Using `shell:true` with an env-var-derived command path is a classic injection vector on Windows. An EMSDK path with spaces or metacharacters (even unintentionally, e.g. a path with `&` from a username) would break or inject. The fix is to use an array form or escape arguments properly.

title: runEmscripten in build-pgo.mjs also uses shell:true for .bat files
description: Identical pattern to security-8 in build-pgo.mjs.
evidence: Duplicate of security-8 in build-pgo.mjs. Same shell:true injection risk via EMSDK env var on Windows.

title: Custom pathToFileUrl does not normalize '../' sequences
description: The local `pathToFileUrl` simply replaces backslashes and prepends `file:///` without normalizing `../` components.
evidence: Confirmed: no `path.resolve()` or normalization of `..` sequences. Compounds security-1 — an attacker-controlled JXL_PGO_MODULE_JS with `../../etc/passwd.js` would produce a valid file:/// URL and be dynamically imported. Should use Node's `pathToFileURL` from `node:url`.

title: No schema validation on the PGO manifest JSON before its fields are used in build commands and file-system operations
description: The `normalizeManifest` function coerces fields with `String()` and `Number()` but applies no bounds checks or allow-lists.
evidence: The `op` field is String-coerced with no allow-list; any value passes through. `name` has no length limit. While no current code path shells out with these values, the lack of validation is a real hardening gap for a build pipeline that consumes external corpus manifests.

title: WASM module loaded from a URL has no SRI integrity verification before compilation
description: loadBrowserModule fetches the WASM binary and compiles it directly without verifying its cryptographic integrity. The build manifest records `wasmIntegrity` but this hash is never used by the loader.
evidence: Code at lines 68-73 confirmed: no integrity option in fetch, no sha384 check before WebAssembly.compile. The `manifest.wasmIntegrity` field exists in the build output but is unused by the loader. A CDN MITM or stale-cache substitution could serve a tampered WASM binary that executes in the browser.

title: Top-level import() in pgo-train.mjs has no try/catch — opaque unhandled rejection on bad module path
description: No try/catch around the top-level `import()` call, so a bad moduleJs path surfaces as an opaque unhandled rejection without identifying which module path was bad.
code_excerpt: const imported = await import(pathToFileUrl(moduleJs));
evidence: Code at line 14 confirmed: bare top-level await import with no surrounding try/catch. The finder correctly pivoted from the false hoisting claim to this real usability bug — a missing module produces an unhandled rejection with no context about which path was attempted.

title: Auto-staging PGO swallows all errors with bare empty catch, masking file-system or JSON parse failures
description: The auto-stage block wraps `stagePgoLock()` in an empty `catch {}`, silently swallowing all errors including JSON parse errors and permission failures.
code_excerpt: } catch {}
evidence: Code at line 129 confirmed: bare `catch {}` swallows everything including genuine failures from `stagePgoLock`. Only ENOENT is the intended silent case. All other errors should at minimum be logged, as they indicate a corrupt PGO corpus that will produce bad profile data.

title: cloneSubmoduleAtCommit silently ignores all errors from runGitCapture HEAD check
description: The `try/catch {}` swallows any error from `runGitCapture` including network errors and permission failures, intending only to catch ENOENT.
evidence: Code at lines 649-651 confirmed: bare `catch {}`. The intent is to handle 'directory doesn't exist yet' (ENOENT), but all other errors are silently swallowed. A permission error or corrupt .git causes silent re-clone with a less informative failure later.

title: linkBridge WebAssembly.validate call does not include the reason from the validator in the error message
description: `WebAssembly.validate(wasmBytes)` is called on the failure branch of `WebAssembly.compile` but the thrown error includes only a boolean result, not the detailed validation message.
evidence: Code confirmed. `WebAssembly.validate` returns a boolean, so the thrown Error only contains the filename, not the validation failure reason. The compile error message is available from the try/catch but not forwarded here. This produces an unhelpful error message when a WASM build fails validation.

title: ensureLibjxlSource catch block silently swallows all errors before the rm+reclone
description: The try/catch catches errors from stat() and .git access, but swallows non-ENOENT errors and proceeds to rm+reclone.
evidence: Code at lines 573-575 confirmed: bare catch swallows everything. A permission denied or I/O error on stat() is indistinguishable from ENOENT, leading to a silent rm -rf of sourceDir and a re-clone attempt that may also fail with a less informative error.

title: encodeRgb16Planar leaks WASM heap allocations when the capability check fails after partial allocation
description: If rPtr succeeds but gPtr throws OOM, rPtr is never freed as there is no try/finally.
evidence: Code at lines 1042-1044 confirmed: three sequential malloc-or-throw calls with no try/finally. If gPtr's `mallocOrThrow` throws OOM, rPtr is leaked. The capability check cleanup at lines 1046-1048 would also fail if bPtr was never allocated. A try/finally block is needed.

title: encodeRgb16Planar leaks rPtr/gPtr/bPtr if takeBuffer throws after the encode call
description: If `takeBuffer` at line 1055 throws, the three plane pointers are never freed as they are freed before the takeBuffer call but takeBuffer can throw.
evidence: Reading the code carefully: the three `_free` calls happen at lines 1052-1054 BEFORE `takeBuffer` is called at line 1055. So by the time takeBuffer throws, rPtr/gPtr/bPtr are already freed. No leak. The finding misread the order of operations.

title: parsePpmHeader in benchmark-pgo.mjs loops forever on a truncated or non-PPM input
description: The `while (tokens.length < 4)` loop does not check `i < bytes.length` at the outer level — on truncated input `i` can reach bytes.length and the inner token loop produces an empty-string token on every iteration, looping forever.
evidence: Code at lines 185-195 confirmed: outer while has no bounds check on `i`. When `i >= bytes.length`, the whitespace skip and comment loops don't execute, the token scan immediately terminates (start===i), an empty string is pushed, and tokens never reaches 4 — infinite loop on truncated or non-PPM input.

title: eventsProgressive: inputClosed error branch throws a decode error when result===0 and inputClosed, but WASM decError may be 0 on a benign flush-complete
description: At lines 1634-1635, when result===0 and inputClosed, code throws `JXL decode error: ${decError(dec)}`. But decError returning 0 means no error — this message would be confusing.
code_excerpt:
} else if (inputClosed) {
          throw new Error(`JXL decode error: ${decError(dec)}`);
evidence: Code at lines 1634-1635 confirmed. If decError returns 0 this produces the misleading message 'JXL decode error: 0'. The condition should additionally check `decError(dec) !== 0` or use a more specific message. This is a real usability bug for diagnostics.

title: deferredRelease pre-allocation throws an Error on OOM instead of falling back to normal transfer path
description: When the 8 MB pre-allocation fails on OOM, a hard throw stops the decoder entirely rather than falling back to the normal transfer path.
evidence: Code at lines 1417-1419 confirmed. The deferredRelease feature is optional/experimental — OOM on the pre-allocation should fall back to `reusablePixelBuf = null` (normal transfer path) with a metric/warning, not reject the entire decode. The hard throw makes deferredRelease unusable on memory-constrained devices.

title: preparePixelsForEmit throws synchronously when pixel data exceeds deferred buffer — buffer should grow instead
description: When pixData.byteLength > reusablePixelCap, throws instead of growing the buffer, which was the facade's own pre-allocation based on an HD estimate.
evidence: Code at lines 1451-1454 confirmed. The 8 MB estimate fails for any image larger than 1920x1080 RGBA8 (e.g. 4K = 33 MB). The buffer should either grow to accommodate the actual frame size or fall back to the copy path. The fixed estimate is the facade's own design choice, so throwing back at the caller is incorrect.

title: compileFromResponse last-ditch fallback silently uses the partially-consumed response body
description: When compileStreaming fails and getFreshResponse is not provided, the code falls through to line 94 and calls `response.arrayBuffer()` on the original, potentially partially-consumed response.
evidence: Code at lines 91-94 confirmed. The comment explicitly acknowledges the partially-consumed risk. `compileStreaming` reads from `response.body`, so calling `arrayBuffer()` on the same response afterward may return empty or partial data, leading to a silent corrupt-WASM compilation error that is hard to diagnose.

title: writeIndexedDbModule is fire-and-forget with a swallowed catch — synchronous throw from IDB factory bypasses the .catch
description: The `.catch(() => {})` handles async rejections but any synchronous throw from the IDB factory call could bypass it and become an unhandled rejection.
evidence: The finder correctly notes that `factory.open` is typically not synchronous-throwing (it returns an IDBOpenDBRequest object). The scenario where it throws synchronously requires a non-standard IDB mock. Cannot confirm or deny from this section alone — depends on test environment details.

title: LibjxlEncoder.chunks() unhelpful error message when no pixels were pushed
description: If finish() is called with queuedPixelBytes===0 and pixelByteTotal>0, the error message 'expected N bytes for rgba8, got 0' is not actionable.
code_excerpt:
if (this.queuedPixelBytes !== this.pixelByteTotal) {
      throw new Error(`JXL encode expected ${this.pixelByteTotal} bytes for ${this.options.format}, got ${this.queuedPixelBytes}`);
evidence: The finder self-assessed as low confidence and concluded this is correct behavior with a suboptimal message. The error IS actionable — it tells the caller exactly how many bytes were expected vs received. The suggestion to add 'encoder received no pixels' is a minor UX improvement, not a bug.

title: mergeProfiles silently succeeds if llvm-profdata is not in PATH
description: No upfront assertion that `llvm-profdata` exists before running the merge step.
code_excerpt: await run("llvm-profdata", ["merge", "-output", profdataPath, ...profrawFiles], { cwd: packageRoot });
evidence: Code confirmed. An ENOENT from spawn gives a low-signal error like 'llvm-profdata: exited with code null'. A clear upfront check with `which llvm-profdata` or a helper would give 'llvm-profdata is required for PGO; install via LLVM toolchain' which is far more actionable.

title: LibjxlEncoder uses console.log for profiling telemetry with no way to silence or route it in production
description: The encoder emits profiling data via `console.log` unconditionally on every encode.
code_excerpt: console.log(`[jxl-wasm-enc] module+create_image setup: ${this.tCreateStart.toFixed(1)}ms`);
evidence: Code confirmed at line 1986 and line 2298 (facade profile summary). Both fire unconditionally on every encode in production browser workers. The `onMetric` callback is present and used for other metrics; these log lines should be routed through it or gated behind a debug flag.

title: decodeTiledRegionRgba8 and decodeTileContainerRegion always console.log timing, no way to suppress in production
description: Both functions emit console.log on every call in the gallery tile decode hot path.
evidence: Code confirmed: unconditional console.log with .toFixed() string formatting in the hot path. For a gallery viewer decoding hundreds of tiles this produces hundreds of console lines per second. Same pattern appears in decodeTileContainerRegion and perceptualConstancyApplyBulk.

title: Only CapabilityMissing has a structured error class; all other WASM errors are plain Error with no code
description: All non-capability errors use plain `new Error(...)` with no code, preventing the scheduler layer from distinguishing retriable vs permanent errors without string-parsing.
evidence: Confirmed: only CapabilityMissing has a typed code. Decode failures, OOM, and invalid dimensions all produce plain Error instances. The decode-handler already matches on `error instanceof CapabilityMissing`; adding OomError and DecodeError classes would let the scheduler layer make informed retry decisions.

title: perceptualConstancyApplyBulk calls WASM AVX2 function with raw JS Float32Arrays instead of WASM heap pointers
description: At line 3153, `fn(r, g, b, targetR, targetG, targetB, n, sat, vib, vibZero ? 1 : 0)` passes JavaScript Float32Array objects directly to a WASM function that expects float* pointer integers.
evidence: Code at line 3153 confirmed: JS Float32Array objects passed directly to a WASM-exported function. WASM functions expect integer heap offsets (pointers), not JS typed array objects. Passing a TypedArray object is coerced by wasm-bindgen/Emscripten glue to its numeric tag value, not a heap address — this silently corrupts memory or produces garbage output whenever `_perceptual_apply_full_avx2` is present in the WASM binary.

title: loadLibjxlModule has a TOCTOU window: concurrent callers can each create a new promise before either stores it
description: The `if (modulePromise === undefined)` check and assignment are not atomic — two concurrent callers can both see undefined and both create fresh promises.
evidence: Code at lines 2375-2383 confirmed. In JS microtask semantics, two callers that both await loadLibjxlModule before either resolves will both enter the if-branch because the check and assignment happen synchronously in the same microtask. The double-load is a spurious redundancy rather than corruption, but if the first load succeeds and the second load fails, the rejection clears the cache abandoning the already-resolved first module instance.

title: LibjxlEncoder.pushPixels chains onto pendingPushPromise but concurrent calls can race on queuedPixelBytes
description: queuedPixelBytes is incremented synchronously before the async push task runs; concurrent pushPixels calls from Promise.all both increment before either task runs.
code_excerpt:
this.queuedPixelBytes += view.byteLength;  // incremented synchronously
  const pushTask = this.pendingPushPromise.then(async () => {
evidence: Code at lines 1929-1933 confirmed. The increment is synchronous and the push is queued via .then(). Two concurrent pushPixels from Promise.all both increment queuedPixelBytes in the same turn. The push-task chain serializes WASM writes correctly, but the double-increment causes chunks() to see a valid queuedPixelBytes value only if both pushes complete — if either is cancelled mid-chain, the byte count is wrong.

title: initModule leaks WASM encoder state when cancel() is called between module load and state creation
description: cancel() fires between `wasmEncState = module._jxl_wasm_enc_create_image!(...)` and `streamingInputActive = true` — wasmEncState is allocated but freeWasmState() might not be called if the caller abandons the encoder.
evidence: Code at lines 2030-2050 confirmed. The cancel() check at line 1984 occurs before state creation. After state is created, a cancel race leaves wasmEncState non-zero with no cleanup until the caller calls cancel() or dispose() on the encoder object — which they may never do if they abandon it after the cancel. Adding a post-creation cancel check with immediate freeWasmState() is the fix.

title: LibjxlEncoder.finish() is silently idempotent but pushPixels after finish() increments queuedPixelBytes before the finished check
description: The finished check in pushPixels is AFTER the byte-count increment — a push-after-finish silently inflates queuedPixelBytes causing chunks() to throw.
code_excerpt:
this.queuedPixelBytes += view.byteLength;
  const pushTask = this.pendingPushPromise.then(async () => {
evidence: Code at line 1924-1933: `if (this.cancelled || this.finished) return` is checked BEFORE the increment, so a post-finish call returns early WITHOUT incrementing. The finder misread the order. However a concurrent pushPixels that reads finished===false before another call sets it to true (impossible in single-threaded JS) is not a real race. This is partially a false positive — but the idempotency asymmetry between finish() and chunks() is still a real undocumented contract issue.

category: shared mutable state / non-thread-safe module-level variable
title: setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting and setForcedTier mutate module-scoped state shared across all decoder/encoder instances in the same worker
description: testModuleFactory, modulePromise, _forcedTier, _cachedDetectedTier are module-level variables mutated by test helpers — a real race if tests are ever parallelised.
code_excerpt:
let modulePromise: Promise<LibjxlWasmModule> | undefined;
let testModuleFactory: JxlModuleFactory | null = null;
evidence: Code at lines 566-574 confirmed. Module-level mutable state shared across all instances. Currently safe because Bun/Vitest runs tests in a single worker sequentially, but the architectural smell is real — parallelizing tests would break this. The pattern also makes test isolation fragile if afterEach cleanup is missed.

title: freeWasmState called from cancel() may call enc_take_chunk(0) on freed state in chunks() generator
description: cancel() zeroes wasmEncState via freeWasmState() while chunks() is suspended at a yield inside enc_take_chunk loop — the next loop iteration checks the now-0 state.
code_excerpt: while ((chunkHandle = module._jxl_wasm_enc_take_chunk!(this.wasmEncState)) !== 0) {
evidence: Code at lines 2100 and 2327-2329 confirmed. freeWasmState() zeroes wasmEncState to 0. The while loop in chunks() reads `this.wasmEncState` on each iteration. After cancel() fires between two yields, `enc_take_chunk(0)` is called with a null/freed state pointer. The C++ behavior of calling enc_take_chunk(0) is undefined — it may crash or return 0 (loop exit), but it is UB.

category: resource leak / WASM heap dangling view
title: takeBufferView returns a HEAPU8.subarray (dangling view) then frees the WASM handle in its finally, invalidating the view before callers can use it
description: `takeBufferView` frees the handle in its finally block, invalidating the HEAPU8.subarray view it returned.
evidence: Code at lines 2603-2616 confirmed. The function returns a live subarray view and then immediately frees the backing WASM memory in the finally. Callers receive a view into freed memory. The comment at line 2599 warns 'only valid for synchronous use in the same tick before any further WASM malloc/grow/realloc' but the freeing in finally makes this even more constrained — any WASM allocation after the call can immediately overwrite the viewed bytes.

title: LibjxlDecoder.cancel() and dispose() rely on generator finally for decFree, but abandoned iterators skip finally
description: If the consumer abandons the events() iterator without calling .return(), the generator's finally block with decFree() never runs, leaking the WASM decoder state.
evidence: Code at lines 1881-1887 confirmed: dispose() does not call decFree directly. If a caller calls dispose() after receiving the final event yield but before the generator returns (or never iterates to completion), the decFree in the generator's finally never executes. WASM has no GC finalizers — the decoder state leaks for the lifetime of the worker.

title: writeIndexedDbModule is fire-and-forget with a swallowed catch, preventing tests from detecting IDB write failures
description: The empty `.catch` swallows logic errors in writeIndexedDbModule that should surface during development.
evidence: Code at lines 74-76 confirmed. The best-effort intent is correct for quota/incognito failures, but swallowing ALL errors including programming mistakes makes the loader harder to test and debug. A DEV-mode rethrow or at minimum a console.warn for non-quota errors would help.

title: loader.ts accepts an AbortSignal but does not plumb it through IDB operations
description: LoaderOptions.signal is passed to fetch but not to readIndexedDbModule or writeIndexedDbModule — abort does not cancel an in-progress IDB read.
code_excerpt: const cached = await readIndexedDbModule(key, options); // ignores signal
evidence: Code at line 58 confirmed: readIndexedDbModule receives options but the IDB open/transaction inside it does not check options.signal. An abort during a cold IDB open will not cancel the IDB operation, only the subsequent fetch. This makes abort latency unpredictable on first cold load.

title: encodeRgba8Pyramid and downscaleRgba16 imported from src/index but do not exist in facade.ts
description: Neither function exists anywhere in the current facade.ts or src/. The runtime tests will fail at import time.
code_excerpt: import { downscaleRgba16, encodeRgba8Pyramid, setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting } from "../src/index";
evidence: Grep of packages/jxl-wasm/src for encodeRgba8Pyramid and downscaleRgba16 returned no matches. The test file at line 2 imports them from ../src/index but they are not implemented. These tests will fail at import time with a TypeError.

title: exports.txt lists many new WASM symbols that have no corresponding TypeScript declarations in facade.ts or LibjxlWasmModule interface
description: ~25 symbols in exports.txt do not appear in the LibjxlWasmModule interface, bypassing type safety.
code_excerpt:
_jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_x
_jxl_wasm_downscale_rgba16
_jxl_wasm_dec_seek_to_frame
_jxl_wasm_encode_with_gain_map
evidence: Confirmed: grep of src/facade.ts found no mention of _jxl_wasm_encode_rgba8_x, _jxl_wasm_downscale_rgba16, _jxl_wasm_dec_seek_to_frame, etc. These are callable WASM exports with no TypeScript declarations, accessible only via `(module as any)._...` with no type checking. New features built on these exports cannot be discovered or type-checked.

title: ButteraugliComparator.compare() is synchronous while create() is async — mixed sync/async contracts at same abstraction level
description: compare() calls mallocOrThrow/WASM synchronously while the rest of the comparator API is async.
code_excerpt: compare(candidate: ArrayBuffer | Uint8Array): number {
evidence: The synchronous compare() is intentional for the hot-path (compare is called in a tight loop), but the asymmetry with async create() is an undocumented contract. The real risk is the stored module reference from construction time — if the module is replaced via setJxlModuleFactoryForTesting, compare() continues to use the old module handle silently.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: encodeTiledRgba8 validates tileSize >= 16 but encodeTileContainer validates tileSize >= 1, inconsistent contract
description: Two public APIs for tiled JXL encoding have different minimum tileSize contracts.
evidence: Code confirmed at lines 889 and 1082. encodeTiledRgba8 enforces >= 16, encodeTileContainer enforces >= 1. A caller using encodeTileContainerRgba8 with tileSize=4 succeeds where encodeTiledRgba8 would throw. The inconsistency can produce valid-looking but decoder-incompatible output depending on which API is used.

title: serializeExtraChannelsForWasm requires two HEAPU8.set calls (before and after plane pointer assignment) but only documents one
description: The two-copy pattern (copy, set plane ptrs in DataView, copy again) is not documented in the function signature, creating a serialization correctness trap.
code_excerpt: // plane_ptr/plane_size left 0 by serialize; filled by caller after malloc
evidence: Code at lines 598 and 623-624 confirms the design: plane_ptr/plane_size are explicitly left 0 in serialize and must be filled by the caller. The JSDoc says this but does not say a second HEAPU8.set is required after filling them. Any caller who omits the second copy sends 0 plane pointers to the WASM bridge, causing silent incorrect encode.

title: EXTRA_TYPE_TO_JXL fallback to 15 for unrecognized ExtraChannelType is silent — new type additions produce 'unknown' without warning
description: The `?? 15` fallback for unrecognized types means future union additions silently encode as 'unknown' (type 15).
code_excerpt: const t = EXTRA_TYPE_TO_JXL[ch.type] ?? 15;
evidence: Code at line 619 confirmed. The Record type annotation means TypeScript would catch missing keys at compile time IF the type is kept in sync — but the `?? 15` fallback at runtime means a value that slips through (e.g. from JSON deserialization) silently produces type 15. The fallback should throw or warn rather than silently downgrade.

title: LibjxlEncoder.finish() is silently idempotent but JxlEncoder interface does not document this
description: finish() is idempotent but chunks() is not — the asymmetry is unspecified in the interface.
evidence: Code confirmed: finish() optional-chains finishResolve (safe on repeat), but chunks() throws on second call. The undocumented asymmetry can mask double-finish bugs in error cleanup paths where finish() is defensively called.

title: DecodeEvent 'budget_exceeded' is missing optional region and sourceScale fields present in 'progress' and 'final' events
description: The 'budget_exceeded' variant only carries stage, info, pixels, format, and pixelStride — missing region?, sourceScale?, progressiveSequence?, passOrdinal?.
evidence: Code at lines 63-69 confirmed: budget_exceeded is missing sourceScale, region, progressiveSequence, passOrdinal. Consumers that handle all pixel-bearing events uniformly must special-case budget_exceeded. The CLAUDE.md documents that budget_exceeded ends the frame stream gracefully, but the different shape makes generic pixel-handler code brittle.

category: missing input validation on public entry point
title: computePsnrWasm and computeSsimWasm do not validate width/height — width=0 passes 0 to _malloc
description: computePsnrWasm and computeSsimWasm compute `pixelSize = width * height * 4` without dimension validation, silently passing 0 to _malloc when width=0.
code_excerpt: const pixelSize = width * height * 4;  // no dimension validation here
evidence: The finder's description correctly identifies that computeButteraugli is guarded via butteraugliPixelSize but computePsnrWasm/computeSsimWasm compute pixelSize inline without guards. `_malloc(0)` on most allocators returns a non-null pointer, so subsequent HEAPU8.set of a 0-length buffer is a no-op — but the downstream WASM perceptual function receives a 0x0 image which may crash or produce undefined results.

title: OOM on metadata malloc silently drops all three metadata boxes (ICC + EXIF + XMP) together even when only one allocation fails
description: When any one metadata malloc fails, all three are freed and the encode proceeds without any metadata, even though others may have succeeded.
evidence: Code at lines 2225-2236 confirmed. A 4-byte EXIF OOM drops the successfully-allocated 16 KB ICC profile. The console.warn is the only signal, and callers using `preserveIcc: true` cannot distinguish this degraded path from a normal encode. The contract violation is real.

category: no contract testing between TypeScript and WASM FFI
title: No runtime contract test verifies that the LibjxlWasmModule interface field layout matches the actual WASM binary exports at load time
description: getCapabilities() probes only ~10 of ~60 declared symbols. A WASM rebuild that drops a symbol would silently fall back with no diagnostic.
code_excerpt: // getCapabilities() only probes ~10 of ~60 symbols
evidence: This is a genuine test infrastructure gap. The exports.txt validation at build time does not prevent runtime divergence when the shipped WASM is from a different build than the TS interface. A post-load `assertWasmContractVersion()` that cross-checks symbol presence would catch build/TS drift immediately.

title: perceptualConstancyApplyBulk falls back to identity (no-op copy) without signalling that no processing occurred
description: When neither WASM perceptual function is present, the function silently copies input to output with no return value, error, or metric emission.
evidence: Code at lines 3171-3174 confirmed. Silent identity fallback when WASM functions are absent. Callers depending on perceptual correction receive unprocessed data with no indication. The onMetric callback is available but no metric is emitted for the identity path. At minimum a console.warn or onMetric('perceptual_apply_skipped', 1) should fire.

title: deferredRelease pre-allocates fixed 8 MB HD-sized buffer that throws on any image larger than 1920x1080
description: Fixed 1920x1080x4 pre-allocation wastes 8 MB for sub-HD images and breaks at runtime for larger images.
code_excerpt: const estimatedPixelBytes = 1920 * 1080 * 4; // ~8 MB, typical HD size
evidence: Code at lines 1411-1415 confirmed. The fixed 8 MB estimate means any decode of a 4K image (33 MB) in deferredRelease mode silently throws via preparePixelsForEmit (errors-11). The buffer should be sized to the actual image dimensions once the header is received, or the initial allocation should be treated as a minimum that grows on demand.

title: takeAndWrap unconditionally copies pixel buffer even when region/downsample is a no-op
description: When applyRegionAndDownsample returns the original pointer unchanged, the code copies the whole frame anyway via `new Uint8Array(buf.data)` even though takeBuffer directly would avoid the copy.
evidence: Code at lines 1468-1470 confirmed. When no region or downsample is applied, pixels.data === buf.data and a full frame copy is performed. For 12 MP RGBA8 this is a ~50 MB copy per frame. Using takeBuffer (which takes ownership and frees) instead of retainBufferView+copy would eliminate this allocation on the common final-only decode path.

title: Unconditional console.log on every decodeTiledRegionRgba8 and decodeTileContainerRegion call
description: Multi-field console.log with .toFixed() string formatting on every call, used in gallery tile decode hot path.
code_excerpt: console.log(`[decodeTiledRegionRgba8] region=${x},${y} size=${w}×${h} ...`);
evidence: Code confirmed at lines 972-977 and equivalent in decodeTileContainerRegion. Unconditional console.log with string interpolation and .toFixed() formatting creates GC pressure on every tile decode. For a gallery viewer with hundreds of tiles this is significant. Same pattern in initModule (line 1986) and perceptualConstancyApplyBulk (line 3156).

title: new TextDecoder() instantiated inside parsePpmHeader loop for each token
description: parsePpmHeader constructs a new TextDecoder() for each token (4 per file) in benchmark loops.
code_excerpt: tokens.push(new TextDecoder().decode(bytes.subarray(start, i)));
evidence: Code at line 194 confirmed. 4 TextDecoder objects per file, per benchmark rep. A module-level shared decoder instance would eliminate all repeated construction. This is a trivial fix with measurable benefit in benchmark loops.

title: scanForValidJpeg has O(n*m) worst-case: for every 0xFF byte it invokes findValidJpegEnd which re-walks up to the range end
description: For a range full of 0xFF bytes, the nested scan produces O(range_size^2) behavior.
evidence: Code at lines 2723-2729 confirmed. For adversarial/malformed input with many 0xFF bytes, each SOI candidate triggers a full forward scan via findValidJpegEnd. The function is called from jxtcSidecars on non-tile ranges — in a heavily corrupted JXTC container this could be slow. The practical risk is low for well-formed data but real for malformed inputs.

category: redundant float multiplications in bilinearResize rgba16/rgbaf32 inner loop
title: bilinearResize rgba16/rgbaf32 paths recompute four bilinear weights per pixel instead of hoisting y-weight multiplication outside the x loop
description: The rgba16/rgbaf32 paths compute w00/w01/w10/w11 from scratch for every pixel; the y-fraction multiplication could be hoisted per row.
evidence: Code at lines 2903-2906 confirmed. `yt` is constant per outer row iteration but `(1-yt)` and `yt` are recomputed for every dx. Hoisting `const oneMinusYt = 1 - yt; const yFrac = yt` outside the dx loop halves the float multiplications for the rgba16/rgbaf32 paths, which matters for large images at 16-bit precision.

category: excessive realloc doubling wastes memory on large encodes
title: EncodeRgbaWithMetadata output buffer doubles on every JXL_ENC_NEED_MORE_OUTPUT which can waste up to 2x the final file size
description: Repeated doublings on NEED_MORE_OUTPUT waste WASM heap for large images.
code_excerpt: outbuf_cap *= 2u;  // doubles on every overflow
evidence: Code at line 729 (and similar in other encode variants) confirmed. A 2× growth factor produces up to 2× final-size waste when initial estimate is small. For mobile WASM environments with 256 MB heap limits, a 10 MB encode could waste 10+ MB of heap. A 1.5× growth factor or using JxlEncoderOutputProcessedBytes would reduce peak allocation.

title: PGO benchmark infrastructure exists but is not wired to a CI performance regression gate
description: compareEncodeBenchmarks has a 2% threshold but no CI step enforces it.
code_excerpt: export function compareEncodeBenchmarks({ baselineMs, candidateMs }) {
evidence: The benchmark and comparison infrastructure is implemented but not wired to CI. Silent performance regressions can land undetected. The 2% threshold and comparison logic already exist — the missing piece is a CI step that runs the benchmarks and fails on regression.
